Q1: I organized my study routine pretty intensively, honestly. With 29 hours a week, I had to be strategic because I'm working with limited resources at home. I'd usually block out chunks of time between my extracurricular activities and make sure I had everything downloaded beforehand since my internet isn't always reliable. I relied heavily on reading and writing things out, so I'd print materials when I could at the library or copy notes into my own documents.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due. I tried to keep some consistency, but with six online courses running simultaneously, I had to be flexible and prioritize based on deadlines. Some weeks I'd be grinding on one course, other weeks I'd shift focus entirely. The low attendance rate doesn't really reflect my effort—I just couldn't always make live sessions work with my schedule.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I needed to check assignment deadlines or download new materials. I'd also log in to submit work since I kept my assignment completion really high. Sometimes I'd check the discussion boards to see if anyone posted questions similar to mine. It became a habit to log in first thing when I had study time blocked out.
Q4: Yeah, logging in became pretty automatic for me, especially around the beginning of each week. I'd check what was posted, download everything I needed, and then plan my offline study time. It happened naturally because I knew I couldn't rely on constant internet access, so I had to be proactive about grabbing materials when I was connected.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me because that's how I learn best. I appreciated when instructors posted detailed notes or transcripts alongside videos. The discussion forums were also helpful because I could read through other students' questions and learn from the answers without always having to ask myself. Anything text-based that I could download and study offline was gold for me.
Q6: I'll be honest, I often skipped the longer video lectures or only watched portions of them. With 90 minutes of video content, I'd skim through to find the key parts rather than watching everything. Some optional readings I also delayed because I was juggling so many courses. If something didn't seem directly connected to assignments, it got pushed to the back burner.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to my assignments or exams. If I knew it would help me complete work or understand concepts I'd be tested on, I prioritized it. I also valued activities where I could engage at my own pace and in my own way—reading and note-taking rather than just passively watching. Efficiency mattered a lot given my time constraints.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they were like a luxury I couldn't always afford. Required activities got done no matter what because my assignment completion was non-negotiable for me. Optional stuff I'd only tackle if I had extra time or if I was genuinely curious about the topic. There was always this calculation in my head about whether the optional work would actually pay off.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs, copy notes into Word documents, and study from printed materials when I could. I also used textbooks and external resources like YouTube tutorials or free online articles to supplement what was in the course. My LMS activity probably looks way lower than my actual study time because so much happened away from the platform.
Q10: There were many times when I was deep in study mode but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd spend hours reviewing downloaded lecture notes, rewriting concepts in my own words, or working through practice problems on paper. Sometimes I'd be at the library without internet, working through materials I'd saved earlier. My best learning often happened when I was disconnected and could focus without distractions.
Q11: All the time. I'd access materials once, download everything I could, and then continue studying them offline for days or even weeks. This was my main strategy because my internet at home isn't always stable. I'd log in maybe once to grab the content, but then I'd review those materials multiple times offline. The single access doesn't reflect how much I actually engaged with the content.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, especially when stress and anxiety built up around mid-semester. I'd feel overwhelmed trying to balance six courses with limited resources. Sometimes I'd feel surprised by how much work piled up, and my mood would drop. The CBT sessions helped me work through some of that, but there were still periods where engagement felt like pushing through mud.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or had competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars, my LMS usage became more minimal and strategic. I'd only log in for essential tasks rather than exploring additional materials. Confusion about course content sometimes made me hesitate to engage because I didn't want to waste time going in circles. I had to be really selective about where I put my energy.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd open materials and my brain just wasn't absorbing anything. I'd be reading the same paragraph over and over because I was too tired or stressed. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open but I was really just going through the motions, not actually learning. Those moments were frustrating because I knew I was putting in time but not getting much out of it.
Q15: I postponed some of the longer readings and video content when I felt overwhelmed. Even knowing they were important, I'd tell myself I'd get to them later because the immediate deadlines took priority. Sometimes anxiety made me avoid things that felt too challenging—I'd procrastinate on starting because I was worried about not understanding. It's something I'm working on recognizing in myself.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my LMS usage. I'd log in more frequently as deadlines approached and my activity would spike around due dates. My whole study schedule revolved around what was due when. I became really good at prioritizing based on urgency, which helped me maintain that high assignment completion rate. Without deadlines, I probably would have engaged differently—more exploratory maybe.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement. When I got positive feedback, it motivated me to keep pushing and engage more with similar materials. Critical feedback sometimes made me anxious, but I tried to use it constructively. I'd go back to the LMS to review related content after getting feedback to make sure I understood where I went wrong. It shaped how I approached subsequent assignments.
Q18: The course structure influenced me a lot. When modules were clearly organized with obvious connections between readings, activities, and assessments, I engaged more smoothly. I appreciated when deadlines were spread out rather than clustered. The structure either made my self-directed studying easier or harder depending on how intuitive the layout was. Clear expectations helped me plan my limited time better.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made engagement way easier for me given my situation. What made it harder was when content was only available through streaming or required constant internet connection. Discussion forums were helpful when they were active, but sometimes they felt dead which was discouraging. The course design worked best for me when it accommodated different learning styles and access situations.
Q20: I think that could happen when students are trying to understand material but struggling. They might be clicking around, rewatching videos, searching for answers but not feeling confident enough to submit work. It could also be anxiety—being active but paralyzed when it comes to actually completing and submitting. Sometimes high activity is actually a sign of confusion rather than productive engagement.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some extent. My attendance was really low but I still performed decently because I was doing most of my learning offline. Someone looking at my LMS data might think I wasn't engaged, but I was putting in serious hours with downloaded materials. The platform only captures one dimension of how students actually learn and study.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you access, submission timing. That's useful for identifying who might be falling behind. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, and the mental effort involved. They can't capture whether I actually understood something or just clicked through it. The emotional and cognitive dimensions of learning are invisible in the data.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively thinking about the material, connecting it to what I already know, and working to understand rather than just complete tasks. It's about genuine curiosity and effort, not just showing up or clicking through. For me, engagement often looks quiet—reading, writing, reflecting—but it's deeply active mentally. It's caring about actually learning, not just getting through.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied offline, the effort I put into handwritten notes, or the hours spent with downloaded materials. It wouldn't capture my emotional journey—the stress, the anxiety, the moments of breakthrough after struggling. It also wouldn't show the peer discussions I had outside the platform or how I used external resources to supplement my learning. My real engagement story is much richer than the data suggests.
